---
# ── FILL IN YOUR DETAILS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
title: "Your Post Title"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-03-18"                         # YYYY-MM-DD format
description: "One sentence. Make it good." # Appears in the post listing
categories: [meditation]                   # Replace with your topic
image: "images/cover.jpg"                  # Optional — delete if no image
bibliography: references.bib
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
toc: true
---

<!-- DELETE THIS BLOCK before submitting ──────────────────────────────────────
FULL TEMPLATE — all optional features included

This template demonstrates all available flourishes. Use the ones that help
your post — delete the ones that don't. None of them are required.

The only things you must have:
  - 800–1000 words of science communication in the third person
  - At least one original visual aid you created
  - In-text citations
  - A full APA reference list at the end
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->


::: {.tldr}
- Key point one — the most surprising or important finding
- Key point two — why it matters
- Key point three — what the reader should take away
:::


## Your opening

![A large image at the top with a caption that sets the scene. Replace with your own.
*(Photo: [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com))*](images/cover.jpg)

Start with something that pulls the reader in. A surprising finding, a counter-intuitive claim, a question worth asking.

Write in the third person. Describe what the researchers found — not what "we" found.


## Background

::: {.callout-note}
## Key concept
Define your central concept here clearly, in plain language.
:::

What is this area of research about? Give the reader enough context to understand why the study matters.

Cite your sources inline: [@authorYear] or [@firstAuthor2020; @secondAuthor2021].


## The study

::: {.pullquote}
"A key sentence from the article or your post — something quotable that captures the core finding."
:::

Describe the specific article you chose. What was the research question? How was the study designed?

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Method
What the researchers did. Who were the participants? What was the procedure?

## Findings
What they found — in plain language. Specific numbers help: "X% of participants..."

## Implications
What these findings mean. Are they in line with other research? What questions remain?

:::


## What does this mean?

![A clear, descriptive caption for your original visual aid.](images/your-figure.png)

Connect the findings to the real world. Why should a non-expert care about this research?

::: {.callout-tip}
## My take
Use this box to clearly separate your interpretation from the evidence.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Limitation to note
Every study has limits. Flag one — sampling, measurement, replication, and so on.
:::


## References

::: {#refs}
:::

---



::: {.author-card}

<div>
<div class="author-card-name">Your Name</div>
<div class="author-card-bio">
  Psychology student at Goldsmiths, University of London.
  Write one sentence about your interests here.
</div>
</div>
:::

*This post is published under [CC BY 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).*
